nativeRange.habitat: Shallow, heavily shaded forest streams, seeps, swampy ditches, and slow-moving blackwater drainage channels in lowland tropical rainforest; typically under thick leaf-litter and root tangles with negligible current

decorPreferences: deep leaf-litter substrate (Indian almond / Catappa leaves)
driftwood and twisted roots for cover and territory delineation
dense low-light planting (Java fern, Cryptocoryne, Bucephalandra, mosses)
floating plants to diffuse surface light and reduce stress
dark or tannin-stained water to replicate blackwater conditions

diet.notes: Micropredator; in the wild feeds on small invertebrates, insect larvae, microcrustaceans, and zooplankton. In captivity readily accepts live and frozen micro-foods: baby brine shrimp, micro-worms, grindal worms, Daphnia, and small bloodworm. High-quality micro-pellets accepted by most specimens once settled; avoid large or rough-textured foods. Feed small amounts 1–2× daily to maintain condition without fouling soft, acid water.

goodWith: blackwater nano fish of similar size
small, peaceful invertebrates (adult Neocaridina/Caridina at caution)
avoidWith: Betta splendens or other aggressive Betta species
other male B. albimarginata (territorial in multiple-male setups)

imageCandidates.notes: FishBase species image for Betta albimarginata; check page for current attribution and licensing before use.

summary: The Albi Betta (Betta albimarginata) is a small wild species from the blackwater forest streams of Borneo, reaching around 3 to 3.5 cm. Unlike most trade bettas, it is peaceful and forms monogamous pairs. Listed as Endangered by the IUCN, it demands very soft, acid water and calm tankmates.
careNotes: Betta albimarginata belongs to the Coccina complex, a group of wild bettas from Borneo's blackwater forest habitats, specifically from Kalimantan (the Indonesian portion of Borneo) and Sabah (on the Malaysian side). Unlike the common trade betta, Betta splendens, this species tends to be peaceful, forms stable monogamous pairs, and does not build bubble nests. Water chemistry is critical: pH should be kept between 4.5 and 6.5, and hardness must remain extremely low. Using reverse osmosis or rainwater buffered with botanicals such as Indian almond (Catappa) leaves, alder cones, and peat is strongly recommended. Hard or alkaline tap water will cause chronic stress and early death. A species-only or carefully curated nano biotope tank of at least 40 L is ideal. Keep the tank tightly covered, as B. albimarginata is a capable jumper. A soft, fine layer of leaf litter provides shelter, a grazing surface for microorganisms, and a natural breeding stimulus. A stable temperature of 24–26°C is recommended as the optimal range within the species' tolerance band. This species is classified as Endangered due to deforestation and habitat loss across Borneo's lowland forests; captive-bred specimens are strongly preferred over wild-caught. Acclimatise slowly, as B. albimarginata is sensitive to sudden changes in water parameters.
breedingNotes: Betta albimarginata is a paternal mouthbrooder. After courtship and spawning (typically in dense planting or under leaf litter), the male collects the fertilised eggs in his mouth and broods them for approximately 10–14 days until fry are free-swimming. The male should not be disturbed during mouthbrooding; feeding may be minimal or suspended. A separate, well-planted breeding tank with the same soft-acid water parameters helps protect the fry. Free-swimming fry are large enough to accept freshly hatched baby brine shrimp and micro-worms from first feeding. The pair typically reforms quickly after brooding. Brood sizes are small (10–30 fry is typical) compared to bubble-nesters. Water temperature towards the upper end of tolerance (26–28°C) can stimulate spawning. Rated moderate-to-difficult due to strict water parameter requirements and the need to protect brooding males.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Betta albimarginata
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided adult size (~3.5 cm), temperature range, pH (4.5–6.5), hardness requirements, native range (Kalimantan and Sabah, Borneo), biotope (blackwater forest streams), mouthbrooding behaviour, diet, compatibility, and care notes. IUCN Endangered status confirmed.

additional.name: FishBase — Betta albimarginata
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (Osphronemidae, Anabantiformes), adult size (~3.5 cm), native range (Borneo: Kalimantan and Sabah), habitat and depth data, and diet classification (invertivore/carnivore).

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Wild Betta Fish Care Guide
additional.notes: Provided trade-oriented care summary for wild Betta species including Coccina complex members; confirmed soft acidic water requirements, feeding on live/frozen micro-foods, and species-appropriate pairing advice.

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — Wild Betta species profiles
additional.notes: Supplementary source confirming paternal mouthbrooding, Coccina complex classification, monogamous pair-bonding behaviour, blackwater biotope, and recommended tank setup. IUCN Endangered status noted.

additional.name: IUCN Red List — Betta albimarginata
additional.notes: Confirmed Endangered (EN) status (2019 assessment). Primary threats: deforestation and habitat degradation in Borneo lowland forest catchments. Supports recommendation for captive-bred stock.

fish.breedingNotes: Betta albimarginata is a paternal mouthbrooder: the male collects and broods fertilised eggs in his mouth for approximately 10 to 14 days. Brood sizes are small, typically 10 to 30 fry. Once free-swimming, the fry are large enough to accept freshly hatched baby brine shrimp and micro-worms from their first feeding. Soft, acid water parameters must be maintained throughout the breeding and rearing period, and the brooding male should be kept undisturbed. Breeding is rated moderate in difficulty due to the strict water chemistry demands and the care required to protect the brooding male.
